children.name: Cnemophilus
children.description: A genus of stout, medium-sized passerines endemic to the montane forests of New Guinea, superficially resembling birds-of-paradise but now placed in their own family. Males are brilliantly coloured in iridescent blues, purples, and yellows, while females are dull olive-brown. Their diet consists primarily of fruit, and they build domed nests in tree forks.

children.name: Cnemophilus loriae
children.description: A medium-sized satinbird with the male a velvety black with a brilliant purplish-blue crown and nape, found in the highlands of Papua New Guinea. It inhabits mid-montane forests at elevations between 1,500 and 3,000 metres, feeding on figs and other forest fruits. Its three-note whistled call is a characteristic sound of the mossy cloud forests.

children.name: Cnemophilus macgregorii
children.description: A striking satinbird in which the male has a brilliant yellow-orange crown and nape contrasting with iridescent blue-black underparts, found in the high mountain forests of New Guinea. It forages in the mid-storey for fruit, often joining mixed-species flocks. Its display involves raising its brightly coloured crown feathers and fluttering between branches.

children.name: Loboparadisea
children.description: A monotypic genus of satinbird endemic to the mountains of New Guinea, with the male possessing a bizarre fleshy yellow wattle around the bill. The genus name meaning 'lobe-paradise bird' reflects its historical misclassification as a bird-of-paradise. It is a frugivore of the mid-montane forests.

children.name: Loboparadisea sericea
children.description: A peculiar satinbird with the male bearing a unique, fleshy yellow wattle above the bill, a golden-yellow breast, and olive-green upperparts, restricted to the central highlands of New Guinea. It feeds on fruit in the subcanopy of montane forests at elevations of 1,500 to 2,800 metres. Little is known of its breeding behaviour, though it is believed to build a cup-shaped nest in tree forks.
